topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official letter lay crumpled on my desk, the ink stark against the cheap paper. My hands trembled as I reread the fine print, the words detailing the award—the prestigious university scholarship—were undeniably mine. But then, a small, almost invisible footnote caught my eye, a section detailing the intended recipient. A name that wasn't mine. A sudden, cold knot tightened in my stomach, squeezing the air from my lungs. I stared at the ceiling, the fluorescent light buzzing overhead seeming to mock my sudden, unwelcome realization. Every successful grade, every late-night study session, suddenly felt tainted, a performance for an audience that wasn't supposed to be watching. I picked up my pen, the plastic feeling alien in my grip, and couldn't bring myself to sign the acceptance form.

[story 2]

It happened during the orientation dinner. I was laughing easily with my classmates, basking in the glow of anticipated success, when Sarah mentioned the scholarship committee. Her tone was casual, almost dismissive, but the words—"It was meant for someone else, you know"—hit me like a physical blow. The noise of the room dissolved into a dull roar. I felt a strange pressure building behind my eyes, a desperate need to disappear. I excused myself abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the floor. Walking back to my dorm room felt like wading through thick mud. I sat on the edge of my bed, tracing the pattern on the worn rug, my breath coming in shallow, quick gasps. I kept replaying the moment, the casual cruelty in her voice echoing in the silence of my room.

[story 3]

The confirmation email arrived at 3 AM. I clicked it open repeatedly, the screen illuminating my pale face in the dark. The details were irrefutable: the financial aid package, the full tuition coverage—it was all mine. Yet, the secondary documentation, the provenance of the funds, pointed toward an application filed under a different name. A slow, creeping sense of wrongness washed over me, heavy and suffocating. I stood up and walked to the window, looking out at the distant city lights. My shoulders slumped forward, a heavy weight settling onto my spine. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the image of the letter away, but the words kept replaying, sharp and insistent, demanding an answer I couldn't give.
